recommendation.root_cause: Null check removed in JWT validation

inputs.alert_text:
ALERT: payment-service — High Error Rate (P1)
Environment: production
Error rate: 71% (up from 0%)

Stack trace:
java.net.SocketTimeoutException: Read timed out after 30000ms calling inventory-service
    at com.example.payment.client.InventoryClient.checkStock(InventoryClient.java:84)
    at com.example.payment.service.PaymentService.processPayment(PaymentService.java:156)
    at com.example.payment.controller.PaymentController.pay(PaymentController.java:72)

Errors began approximately 24 minutes ago.
Affected endpoints: POST /v1/payments (71% failure rate)

triage.error_message: Read timed out after 30000ms calling inventory-service
triage.reasoning: The alert explicitly names payment-service as the affected service with a 71% error rate on POST /v1/payments. The stack trace shows a SocketTimeoutException originating from InventoryClient.checkStock(), which is timing out after 30s when calling inventory-service. This strongly indicates that inventory-service is degraded or unresponsive, causing cascading failures in payment-service. The 30s timeout is notably long and suggests payment-service lacks aggressive circuit-breaking. The root cause is almost certainly in inventory-service, but the alerting service experiencing the errors is payment-service.

recommendation.root_cause: Deployment commit-52f8e1940 configured inventory-service HTTP client timeout at 30000ms (in PaymentServiceConfig.java/InventoryClient.java), which is below inventory-service's normal P95 latency of 28–32s, causing 71% of payment requests to fail with SocketTimeoutException when inventory-service latency spiked during peak traffic.
recommendation.recommended_fix: Immediately apply the feature flag override to set the inventory timeout to 60000ms: ff.payment.inventory_timeout_override=60000. Then prepare a hotfix to permanently set inventory.http.timeout.ms=60000 in PaymentServiceConfig.java. As a follow-up, enable a circuit breaker on the inventory-service dependency to prevent cascading failures.
contributing_factors: inventory.http.timeout.ms set to 30000ms instead of the required ≥60000ms, exactly repeating the misconfiguration from INC-4821

inventory-service itself is experiencing elevated latency (P95=28900ms, P99=34500ms), with 71% of requests exceeding the 30s timeout — suggesting inventory-service is also partially degraded beyond its normal baseline
No circuit breaker configured on the inventory-service dependency, allowing cascading failures to saturate payment-service thread pools with 30s-hanging requests
30s timeout is excessively long for a synchronous blocking call, tying up payment-service resources and amplifying the blast radius to upstream consumers (api-gateway, checkout-service, mobile-api)
The deployment occurred ~10 hours before errors started, meaning the latent misconfiguration was exposed only when inventory-service latency shifted upward during a peak traffic period
recommendation.escalation_reason: P1 incident actively impacting 71% of all payment transactions in production. Additionally, inventory-service itself appears degraded (71% failure rate implies latency well beyond its normal P95 of 28-32s), so the inventory-service team should be paged to investigate their own service health in parallel. The timeout fix will reduce payment-service errors but won't address the underlying inventory-service degradation.

inputs.alert_text:
ALERT: payment-service — High Error Rate (P1)
Environment: production
Error rate: 71% (up from 0%)

Stack trace:
java.net.SocketTimeoutException: Read timed out after 30000ms calling inventory-service
    at com.example.payment.client.InventoryClient.checkStock(InventoryClient.java:84)
    at com.example.payment.service.PaymentService.processPayment(PaymentService.java:156)
    at com.example.payment.controller.PaymentController.pay(PaymentController.java:72)

Errors began approximately 24 minutes ago.
Affected endpoints: POST /v1/payments (71% failure rate)

triage.error_message: Read timed out after 30000ms calling inventory-service
triage.reasoning: The alert explicitly names payment-service with a P1 high error rate of 71%. The stack trace shows a SocketTimeoutException originating from InventoryClient.checkStock(), meaning payment-service is timing out waiting for a response from inventory-service after 30 seconds. The call chain is clear: PaymentController -> PaymentService.processPayment -> InventoryClient.checkStock, which fails on the downstream call to inventory-service. The root cause is almost certainly inventory-service being degraded or unresponsive, causing cascading failures in payment-service. Immediate investigation should focus on inventory-service health (CPU, memory, latency, recent deployments) while also considering adding or tuning circuit breakers in payment-service to fail fast rather than holding connections for 30s.

recommendation.root_cause: Deployment commit-52f8e1940 configured the inventory-service HTTP client timeout to 30000ms, which is below inventory-service's normal P95 latency of 28-32s, causing widespread SocketTimeoutExceptions once inventory-service latency naturally fluctuated above the threshold.
recommendation.recommended_fix: Immediately set inventory.http.timeout.ms to 60000ms using the feature flag ff.payment.inventory_timeout_override=60000 for instant relief without a redeploy. Then follow up with a hotfix commit reverting the timeout configuration in PaymentServiceConfig.java to 60000ms, and implement a circuit breaker on the inventory-service call path.
contributing_factors: inventory-service timeout set to 30000ms in PaymentServiceConfig.java, below the required ≥60000ms threshold documented in runbook RB-1042
inventory-service inherently has high P95 latency (28900ms in current traces, historically 28-32s per INC-4821), making a 30s timeout marginal and fragile
No circuit breaker configured on the inventory-service dependency, so every timed-out request blocks for the full 30s and cascading failures are not mitigated
11-hour gap between deploy and incident onset suggests inventory-service latency was marginally under 30s during low-traffic hours but crossed the threshold as daytime load increased

inputs.alert_text:
ALERT: order-service — DB Connection Pool Exhausted (P1)
Environment: production
Error rate: 89% (up from 0%)

Exception:
com.zaxxer.hikari.pool.HikariPool$PoolInitializationException:
  HikariPool-1 - Connection is not available, request timed out after 30000ms
    at com.example.order.repository.OrderRepository.save(OrderRepository.java:43)
    at com.example.order.service.OrderService.createOrder(OrderService.java:112)

Errors began approximately 61 minutes ago.
Affected endpoints: POST /v1/orders (89% failure rate)

triage.error_message: HikariPool-1 - Connection is not available, request timed out after 30000ms
triage.reasoning: The alert explicitly names order-service with a P1 DB connection pool exhaustion. The stack trace shows a HikariCP pool timeout exception originating from OrderRepository.save(), confirming the service is unable to acquire database connections. The 30-second timeout and 89% error rate on POST /v1/orders indicate the downstream database (or its connection pool) is the bottleneck — likely caused by the database being unresponsive, slow queries holding connections, or a connection leak. The downstream dependency is the order database backing OrderRepository, though the specific database hostname is not provided in the trace.

recommendation.root_cause: Deployment v3.2.0 increased Tomcat max threads from 50 to 200 and removed request queuing in OrderService.java, but the HikariCP database connection pool size was left at 10, causing up to 200 concurrent threads to contend for 10 DB connections and exhaust the pool.
recommendation.recommended_fix: Immediately increase spring.datasource.hikari.maximum-pool-size from 10 to 50 (matching the proven fix from INC-5102), and redeploy. If the database cannot handle 50 connections, alternatively rollback to v3.1.x to restore the 50-thread limit and request queuing. Long-term, set pool size proportional to thread count (e.g., pool_size = threads / 4 with queuing) and add a circuit breaker on the DB dependency.
recommendation.fix_code_snippet:
# application.properties — immediate fix
server.tomcat.threads.max=200
spring.datasource.hikari.maximum-pool-size=50
spring.datasource.hikari.minimum-idle=10
spring.datasource.hikari.connection-timeout=10000

# Alternatively, full rollback to v3.1.x:
# server.tomcat.threads.max=50
# spring.datasource.hikari.maximum-pool-size=10
contributing_factors: Tomcat max threads increased 4x (50→200) without a corresponding increase to HikariCP maximum-pool-size (still 10)
Request queuing logic was removed from OrderService.java, eliminating backpressure and allowing all 200 threads to hit the DB concurrently
No circuit breaker configured on the order-db JDBC dependency to shed load when the pool is saturated
No pre-deployment validation or load testing to catch the thread-to-connection ratio imbalance
30-second HikariCP connection timeout causes threads to block for a long time before failing, compounding the exhaustion

inputs.alert_text:
ALERT: notification-service — Kafka Consumer Lag Critical (P2)
Environment: production
Consumer lag on topic order.confirmed: 187,400 messages (was 320 one hour ago)

Error in logs:
Consumer thread blocked for 45000ms in EmailSenderService.sendWithRetry()
  — possible blocking I/O on HTTP call to delivery-tracker-api

Lag spike began approximately 53 minutes ago.
Messages are not being processed. Notification backlog growing at ~3500/min.

triage.error_message: Kafka consumer lag critical on topic order.confirmed (187,400 messages). Consumer thread blocked for 45000ms in EmailSenderService.sendWithRetry() due to blocking HTTP call to delivery-tracker-api. Notification backlog growing at ~3500/min.
triage.reasoning: The alert explicitly names notification-service as the affected service. The root cause is a blocking HTTP call to delivery-tracker-api within EmailSenderService.sendWithRetry(), which is stalling the Kafka consumer thread. Because the consumer cannot make progress, lag on the order.confirmed topic has exploded from 320 to 187,400 messages in ~53 minutes. The downstream dependency is clearly delivery-tracker-api — either it is slow, unresponsive, or timing out, causing the synchronous retry logic to block the consumer thread for 45 seconds at a time. Confidence is very high as both the service and dependency are explicitly identified in the alert and logs.

recommendation.root_cause: Deployment v2.5.3 (commit 9b2e7a33f) introduced a synchronous blocking HTTP call to delivery-tracker-api inside EmailSenderService.sendWithRetry(), which executes on the Kafka consumer thread with a 45-second timeout, causing the consumer to stall on every message and producing catastrophic lag on the order.confirmed topic.
recommendation.recommended_fix: Immediate: Roll back to v2.5.2 to restore consumer throughput and drain the 187K message backlog. Follow-up: Reimplement the delivery confirmation feature using an async pattern (CompletableFuture or a separate thread pool) so the Kafka consumer thread is never blocked by HTTP I/O. Add a circuit breaker on delivery-tracker-api calls. Ensure Kafka message acknowledgment is not gated on the HTTP response.
contributing_factors: Synchronous HTTP call with 45s timeout placed directly on the Kafka consumer poll thread, blocking message processing
delivery-tracker-api has P95 latency of 40-50 seconds, meaning nearly every call hits the timeout ceiling
No circuit breaker configured for the delivery-tracker-api dependency, so every message incurs the full timeout
Consumer ack is gated on HTTP response completion — no async decoupling between consumption and HTTP I/O
No pre-deployment load testing or review gate caught the blocking I/O anti-pattern on the consumer thread

inputs.alert_text:
ALERT: auth-service — Login Failures Spiking (P1)
Environment: production
Error rate: 52% (up from 0%)

Stack trace:
java.lang.NullPointerException: Cannot invoke String.isEmpty() on null token
    at com.example.auth.service.UserService.validateToken(UserService.java:88)
    at com.example.auth.filter.JwtAuthFilter.doFilterInternal(JwtAuthFilter.java:54)
    at org.springframework.web.filter.OncePerRequestFilter.doFilter(OncePerRequestFilter.java:117)

Errors began approximately 38 minutes ago.
Affected endpoints: POST /v1/auth/login (52% failure rate)

triage.error_message: Cannot invoke String.isEmpty() on null token
triage.reasoning: The alert explicitly names auth-service with a P1 login failure spike. The stack trace shows a NullPointerException in UserService.validateToken where a token string is null when String.isEmpty() is called on it. This is an internal bug in the auth-service's JWT validation logic (JwtAuthFilter -> UserService.validateToken) — likely a missing null check on the token before calling isEmpty(). No downstream dependency is implicated; the failure occurs before any external call is made. The sudden onset (0% to 52% error rate ~38 minutes ago) suggests a recent deployment or configuration change may have introduced a code path where the token is not being populated correctly (e.g., a change in request format, header name, or token extraction logic).

recommendation.root_cause: Deployment v1.9.1 (commit f4c8a211d) removed the null-safety guard on the token parameter in UserService.validateToken() at line 88, causing a NullPointerException when clients (notably mobile) send requests with a null token value.
recommendation.recommended_fix: Immediately roll back to v1.9.0 (commit b3e9c441a) to restore the null-check guard. Then apply a hotfix to v1.9.1 that restores the null check: change line 88 from 'if (token.isEmpty()) return false;' back to 'if (token == null || token.isEmpty()) return false;'. Deploy the hotfix after adding a unit test for the null-token case.
contributing_factors: Deployment v1.9.1 explicitly removed 'token == null' check from the guard clause in UserService.java lines 85-90
Some clients (mobile-api, web-app) legitimately send requests where the token field/header is null or absent, which the previous code handled gracefully
No unit test or integration test caught the null-token regression before deployment
No pre-deployment static analysis or null-safety linting was enforced to flag the unsafe String.isEmpty() call on a nullable field

    at com.example.auth.service.UserService.validateToken(UserService.java:88)
    at com.example.auth.filter.JwtAuthFilter.doFilterInternal(JwtAuthFilter.java:54)
    at org.springframework.web.filter.OncePerRequestFilter.doFilter(OncePerRequestFilter.java:117)

Errors began approximately 38 minutes ago.
Affected endpoints: POST /v1/auth/login (52% failure rate)

triage.error_message: Cannot invoke String.isEmpty() on null token
triage.reasoning: The alert explicitly names auth-service with a P1 login failure spike. The stack trace shows a NullPointerException in UserService.validateToken where a token variable is null when String.isEmpty() is called on it. This is an internal bug in the auth-service's JWT validation logic (JwtAuthFilter -> UserService.validateToken) — no downstream dependency is implicated. The null token suggests either the login flow is failing to generate/retrieve a token before validation, or a recent code deployment introduced a regression that fails to handle a null token case. The 52% error rate starting abruptly 38 minutes ago is consistent with a bad deployment or configuration change affecting a subset of requests.

recommendation.root_cause: Deployment v1.9.1 (commit f4c8a211d) removed the null-safety guard (`token == null`) from UserService.validateToken() at line 88, causing a NullPointerException when mobile or unauthenticated clients send requests with a null token value.
recommendation.recommended_fix: Immediately rollback to v1.9.0 or deploy a hotfix restoring the null-check guard: change `if (token.isEmpty()) return false;` back to `if (token == null || token.isEmpty()) return false;` in UserService.java line 88. This is the exact same resolution as past incident INC-3841.
contributing_factors: Deployment v1.9.1 explicitly removed the `token == null` null-check from the JWT validation guard clause in UserService.java lines 85-90
Approximately 52% of incoming login requests arrive with a null token (likely from mobile clients or unauthenticated initial login attempts that pass through the JwtAuthFilter before a token exists)
No null-safety static analysis or unit test coverage existed to catch the regression before deployment
An identical regression occurred previously (INC-3841, 2024-07-08) indicating the lesson was not codified into CI/CD guardrails

Environment: production
Error rate: 71% (up from 0%)

Stack trace:
java.net.SocketTimeoutException: Read timed out after 30000ms calling inventory-service
    at com.example.payment.client.InventoryClient.checkStock(InventoryClient.java:84)
    at com.example.payment.service.PaymentService.processPayment(PaymentService.java:156)
    at com.example.payment.controller.PaymentController.pay(PaymentController.java:72)

Errors began approximately 24 minutes ago.
Affected endpoints: POST /v1/payments (71% failure rate)

triage.error_message: Read timed out after 30000ms calling inventory-service
triage.reasoning: The alert explicitly names payment-service as the affected service with a 71% error rate on POST /v1/payments. The stack trace shows a SocketTimeoutException originating from InventoryClient.checkStock(), which times out after 30s when calling inventory-service. This strongly indicates that inventory-service is degraded or unreachable, causing cascading failures in payment-service. The root cause is almost certainly in inventory-service (downstream), but the alerting service experiencing the errors is payment-service. Confidence is 98% rather than 100% because while the immediate cause is clear, the ultimate root cause within inventory-service (e.g., its own downstream dependency, resource exhaustion, deployment issue) is not yet determined.

recommendation.root_cause: The inventory-service HTTP client timeout is configured at 30000ms (should be ≥60000ms per runbook RB-1042), and inventory-service P95 latency of 28900ms causes ~71% of requests to exceed this threshold during normal-to-elevated load conditions.
recommendation.recommended_fix: Immediately set inventory.http.timeout.ms to 60000ms via feature flag ff.payment.inventory_timeout_override=60000 (fastest path, no deploy needed). Then follow up with a hotfix deployment to correct the timeout in PaymentServiceConfig.java. Error rate should drop to 0% within 90 seconds based on INC-4821 resolution.

No circuit breaker configured for the blocking inventory-service dependency, allowing cascading failures to propagate unchecked
inventory-service baseline latency (P95=28900ms, P99=34500ms) is inherently close to the 30s timeout boundary, making payment-service fragile to any latency variance
30-second timeout per request causes thread pool exhaustion in payment-service, amplifying the impact beyond just the slow requests

inputs.alert_text:
ALERT: dynamodb-service — Critical Service Degradation (P0)
Environment: production (us-east-1)
Error rate: 94% (up from 0%)

Error:
java.net.UnknownHostException: dynamodb.us-east-1.amazonaws.com
    at java.net.InetAddress.getAllByName0(InetAddress.java:1281)
    at com.amazonaws.internal.ConnectionUtils.connectToEndpoint(ConnectionUtils.java:47)
    at com.amazonaws.services.dynamodbv2.AmazonDynamoDBClient.invoke(AmazonDynamoDBClient.java:4892)

Errors began approximately 37 minutes ago.
Affected services: ALL services using DynamoDB (session-service, user-service, auth-service, order-service)
Impact: Complete inability to read/write to DynamoDB tables
AWS Health Dashboard: Investigating increased error rates for DynamoDB in US-EAST-1

NOTE: This scenario is based on the real AWS October 2025 outage caused by a DNS race 
condition that deleted all DynamoDB endpoint IP addresses, affecting 113 AWS services 
for 15 hours. Root cause: DNS planner/enactor race condition in automated DNS management.

triage.error_message: java.net.UnknownHostException: dynamodb.us-east-1.amazonaws.com — DNS resolution failure for DynamoDB endpoint, causing complete inability to read/write to DynamoDB tables. 94% error rate across all dependent services (session-service, user-service, auth-service, order-service).
triage.reasoning: The stack trace is unambiguous: UnknownHostException on dynamodb.us-east-1.amazonaws.com means DNS cannot resolve the DynamoDB service endpoint to any IP address. This is NOT an application bug or a network connectivity issue in the traditional sense — it is a DNS-level failure at the AWS infrastructure layer. The alert explicitly names 'dynamodb-service' as the affected service, and the cascading impact to session-service, user-service, auth-service, and order-service confirms DynamoDB is the shared downstream dependency. The AWS Health Dashboard note about investigating increased error rates, combined with the note referencing the real AWS October 2025 DNS race condition (where an automated DNS planner/enactor race deleted all DynamoDB endpoint IP records), strongly indicates this is an AWS-side regional outage rather than anything the customer can fix. Mitigation options are extremely limited: DNS caching at the client/resolver level might temporarily help if cached TTLs haven't expired, and multi-region failover to another AWS region (if architected) is the primary remediation path. Confidence is 97% rather than 100% because in theory a local DNS resolver failure or VPC DNS configuration issue could produce the same exception, but the 94% error rate across all services plus the AWS Health Dashboard confirmation rules that out.

recommendation.root_cause: AWS-side DNS infrastructure failure: an automated DNS planner/enactor race condition in AWS's internal DNS management system deleted all A-record IP addresses for the dynamodb.us-east-1.amazonaws.com endpoint, causing UnknownHostException for all DynamoDB operations in us-east-1.
recommendation.recommended_fix: IMMEDIATE (minutes 0-15): 1) Confirm AWS Health Dashboard status and open AWS Support case at Severity 1. 2) Set JVM DNS cache TTL to extend cached resolutions: -Dsun.net.inetaddress.ttl=300 and restart services that still have cached IPs. 3) If any service instances still have cached DNS, route all traffic to those instances. 4) Add static /etc/hosts entries on application hosts mapping dynamodb.us-east-1.amazonaws.com to known DynamoDB IP ranges (check last known IPs from DNS logs). MEDIUM-TERM (hours): 5) If multi-region architecture exists, failover DynamoDB Global Tables to us-west-2 and redirect traffic. 6) For stateless reads, enable DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) in an alternate region. LONG-TERM: Implement multi-region active-active with Global Tables and automated regional failover.
recommendation.fix_code_snippet:
# Immediate mitigation: Static DNS override on application hosts
# Add to /etc/hosts on all application servers (use last-known IPs from DNS cache/logs)
# WARNING: Use only as emergency measure until AWS restores DNS

# Option 1: /etc/hosts override
52.94.x.x dynamodb.us-east-1.amazonaws.com
52.94.x.y dynamodb.us-east-1.amazonaws.com

# Option 2: JVM DNS caching (add to JVM startup flags)
# -Dsun.net.inetaddress.ttl=300
# -Dsun.net.inetaddress.negative.ttl=0

# Option 3: Application-level DynamoDB client with endpoint override to us-west-2
# application.yml
aws:
  dynamodb:
    endpoint-override: https://dynamodb.us-west-2.amazonaws.com
    region: us-west-2
    global-table-failover: true

# Option 4: Add circuit breaker to DynamoDB calls (Resilience4j)
resilience4j:
  circuitbreaker:
    instances:
      dynamodb:
        failureRateThreshold: 50
        waitDurationInOpenState: 30s
        slidingWindowSize: 20
        permittedNumberOfCallsInHalfOpenState: 5
        recordExceptions:
          - java.net.UnknownHostException
          - com.amazonaws.SdkClientException
contributing_factors: AWS internal DNS automation race condition between DNS planner and enactor components that incorrectly deleted all DynamoDB endpoint IP records
All application services are deployed in a single region (us-east-1) with no multi-region failover capability for DynamoDB
No client-side DNS caching with extended TTL configured in the JVM or application layer, meaning cached entries expired quickly after AWS DNS records were deleted
No circuit breaker or graceful degradation pattern implemented — services fail at 94% rather than shedding load and serving partial/cached responses
AWS SDK default retry configuration does not account for DNS-level failures, retrying against the same unresolvable hostname
recommendation.escalation_reason: This is an AWS regional infrastructure outage affecting DNS resolution for DynamoDB in us-east-1. The root cause is entirely within AWS's control (DNS planner/enactor race condition). Escalation is required to: (1) AWS Support at Severity 1 for direct engagement with the DynamoDB service team, (2) Internal VP/Director-level leadership given complete production impact across all services, (3) Internal platform/infrastructure team to execute multi-region failover if available. Based on the referenced October 2025 AWS outage, resolution took approximately 15 hours — extended executive communication and customer-facing status updates are needed.

inputs.alert_text:
ALERT: payment-service — High Error Rate (P1)
Environment: production
Error rate: 71% (up from 0%)

Stack trace:
java.net.SocketTimeoutException: Read timed out after 30000ms calling inventory-service
    at com.example.payment.client.InventoryClient.checkStock(InventoryClient.java:84)
    at com.example.payment.service.PaymentService.processPayment(PaymentService.java:156)
    at com.example.payment.controller.PaymentController.pay(PaymentController.java:72)

Errors began approximately 24 minutes ago.
Affected endpoints: POST /v1/payments (71% failure rate)

triage.error_message: Read timed out after 30000ms calling inventory-service
triage.reasoning: The alert explicitly names 'payment-service' as the affected service. The stack trace shows a java.net.SocketTimeoutException thrown at InventoryClient.checkStock(), meaning payment-service is failing because calls to the downstream 'inventory-service' are not responding within the 30-second read timeout. The call chain is clear: PaymentController.pay() → PaymentService.processPayment() → InventoryClient.checkStock() → timeout. The 71% error rate on POST /v1/payments correlates directly with this synchronous blocking dependency on inventory-service. The root cause is almost certainly inventory-service being slow, unresponsive, or down — not a bug in payment-service itself. Recommended immediate actions: (1) Check inventory-service health/latency metrics, (2) Consider circuit-breaking or fallback logic in InventoryClient, (3) Investigate inventory-service infra for resource exhaustion or deployment issues starting ~24 minutes ago.

recommendation.root_cause: Deployment 25 minutes ago changed inventory.http.timeout.ms from 60000 to 30000 ms. inventory-service P95 latency is 28-32s under load, exceeding the new timeout.
recommendation.recommended_fix: Revert inventory.http.timeout.ms to 60000 in application.properties and redeploy (hotfix). Alternatively use feature flag ff.payment.inventory_timeout_override=60000 for immediate relief.
recommendation.fix_code_snippet:
# application.properties
inventory.http.timeout.ms=60000
# OR feature flag (no deploy needed):
ff.payment.inventory_timeout_override=60000
contributing_factors: Deploy v2.14.3 reduced HTTP timeout from 60 000 ms → 30 000 ms
inventory-service P95 latency is 28-32s under current load
No circuit-breaker configured for InventoryClient
This exact pattern occurred in INC-4821 (Sep 2024)
